content:
,199
Welcome to the first lesson in ICT bond trading Concepts. When dealing with commodities, it's important to
remember that I am not a licensed CTA.
- This is not trade advice.
- Everything referred to in this month's content and these lessons is for paper trade only.

,320
This lesson is part of the June 2017 ICT Mentorship, focusing on ICT bond trading lesson one: Basics and
the Opening Range Concept.

section_title: DEFINING THE TREASURY BOND FUTURES CONTRACT
content:
,020
When referring to the treasury bond, I am specifically referring to the 30-year treasury bond Futures
Contract.
- Trade Symbol: ZB
- Trading Session for Concepts: Primarily the New York session.
- New York Session Time: 8:20 A.M. to 3:00 P.M. New York time.

section_title: CONTRACT DELIVERY MONTHS AND SYMBOL FORMAT
content:
,799
The contract delivery months for the 30-year treasury bond note are:
- March (Contract Code: H)
- June (Contract Code: M)
- September (Contract Code: U)
- December (Contract Code: Z)
The format for entering charts or pulling data is: ZB + Month Code + Last two digits of the trading year.
- Example: ZBU17 for September 2017's contract of the 30-year treasury bond.
- Trades on the Chicago Board of Trade exchange as a Futures Contract.

section_title: TICK VALUE AND FULL HANDLE DEFINITION
content:
,120
The amount per tick (minimum fluctuation) is 31.25 per contract.
- A full handle or full figure move equals 32 ticks, or is referred to as 30 seconds.
- 32 ticks movement equals 1,000 dollars per contract.

,099
The Bond opening range concept is a fundamental element in commodity trading.
- Highest volume is typically seen between 8 A.M. and 9:30 A.M. New York time.
- The 'true day' for the bond market is 8 A.M. to 3 P.M. New York time.
- The opening range begins at 8 A.M. New York time and ends at 9 A.M. New York time.
- This 8 A.M. to 9 A.M. opening range tends to create the bond market high or low of the day.

,220
The opening range (8 A.M. to 9 A.M.) can be:
- A run-on stops.
- A fair value setup (bullish order block or bearish order block).
- A trade down into a liquidity void or fill in a fair value gap.
- It is also the location for liquidity pools to build around for the Stock Market opening to be rated.

section_title: EXAMPLE 1: OPENING RANGE LOW AND STOP RUN
content:
,739
Consider a 15-minute Candlestick chart of the ZBU17 (September 2017 treasury bond contract), with the 8
A.M. to 9 A.M. period delineated.
- We identify the opening range high and low within this period.
- Price trades down below the low formed between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M.
- This action is a stop run and a potential reversal.

,900
Notice the highest volume of the day is typically between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M.
- The lower low created post-9 A.M. New York time was seen with lower volume.
- This is a volume divergence: price making a lower low should ideally be met with a higher bar on volume.
- Lower volume on a lower low is a sign of impending weakness for the down move, indicating no more
selling pressure; it's basically a run on stops.
- Price subsequently moves higher, finding support at the opening range, and continues trading higher into
the latter portions of the day.

section_title: EXAMPLE 2: BULLISH ORDER BLOCK WITHIN OPENING RANGE
content:
,280
Another example using the 8 A.M. to 9 A.M. price action block to define the opening range high and low.
- Inside the opening range, identify a bullish order block (the last two down-closed candles).
- Price trades down into this bullish order block around the 8 A.M. to 9 A.M. opening range concept.

section_title: EXAMPLE 2: VOLUME DIVERGENCE ON A RALLY
content:
,280
Price rallies away from the bullish order block.
- As price makes a higher high going into the 11 o'clock hour, volume was declining.
- This volume divergence was an early sign that the rally up into 154.21 was a weak move.
- Ideally, volume should have had a higher bar on its highest high moving up to 154.21.
- Volume precedes price; weakness was seen after 154.21 had posted, leading to a subsequent decline
back into the opening range and consolidation.

section_title: EXAMPLE 3: BULLISH ORDER BLOCK AND STOP RUN
content:
,240
Using the 8 A.M. to 9 A.M. delineation, we define our opening range high and low.
- Inside the opening range, the last down-closed candle is a bullish order block.
- There are two reference points for testing this order block, both fitting the bill.
- With a bullish order block, we expect a rally. The largest volume was seen again between 8 A.M. and 9
A.M. New York time.

section_title: EXAMPLE 3: DIVERGENCE, STOP HUNT, AND FAIR VALUE GAP
content:
,339
Price moves up, running into the 154.26 level.
- Notice that the high formed around 154.26, before 2 P.M., was not seen with greater volume when
measured later in the day/evening.
- The move to 154.26 was done on light volume, and with the divergence in volume, it reached above the
opening range high.
- This indicates it ran the stops above that range, failed to go any higher, and traded down to close in the
fair value gap seen at the 154.10 level.
- This blends concepts from Forex mentorship with specific commodities.

- BLENDING CONCEPTS
,860
For bonds, I define the opening range similar to the Asian range in Forex:
- Incorporate the bodies of candles.
- Incorporate wicks.
- Incorporate previous highs and lows that are just to the left of the 8 A.M. to 9 A.M. New York time period.
For instance, highs formed between 2 A.M. and 12 A.M. to the left of the chart (equal highs just below
154.19) can be incorporated with the wick between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M.
- This reference point can become an issue for stop rating later during the Asian session, showing how we
blend concepts.

section_title: OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY TIMES FOR BONDS
content:
,640
I prefer to see signals form at 8:20 A.M. or after, which is around the CME opening.
- Signals can occur as early as 8 A.M. or sometimes a little before, similar to the New York session open at
7 A.M.
- Generally, I prefer to see signals occur between 8 A.M. and 8:30 A.M.
- This period sees a strong influx of New York traders, with London traders still participating, making it a
very liquid market.

section_title: BOND MARKET MANIPULATION AND NEWS EVENT AVOIDANCE
content:
,000
The bond market is noted as the least manipulated of all markets, though not entirely free of manipulation.
- On par with other asset classes, it generally sees the least manipulation.
- During FOMC, interest rate reports, or other significant news events (like Non-Farm Payroll), the bond
market can become 'wonky' or 'silly', exhibiting illogical price action.
- The easiest way to avoid this is to stay out of the asset class ahead of these news events.
- Trading around these times is illiquid, and the market is likely to gap against your position. It's best not to
trade at all during these periods.

section_title: ADVANTAGES OF TRADING THE BOND MARKET
content:
,380
If you wish to focus on a single market, the bond market offers a plethora of opportunities:
- Highly liquid.
- Very nice when trending; generally stays in one direction once it moves.
- Can be choppy in consolidation, but if you know fair value gaps, Turtle soups, or equilibrium ideas, you
can capture ticks.
- Realistic daily range: capturing 5-8 ticks intraday is good (16/32nds = 500 dollars per contract).
- A large range day (full handle or 32 ticks = 1,000 dollars) doesn't happen all the time, but sustained
moves can create it.
- Allows for low expectations in daily range.
- Respects liquidity pools, liquidity gaps, and fair value gaps, similar to Forex, but without the extreme
'crazy stuff' spiking.
- Not a perfect market, but if Forex became untradeable, I would return to bonds without hesitation. It's a
very good trading market.

section_title: BOND MARKET TRADING STYLES AND DAILY PRACTICE
content:
,620
The bond market can be:
- Swing traded.
- Short-term traded.
- Day traded.
- Held for longer terms (several months) when conditions are right, as it trends very well like a currency.
I strongly recommend keeping a running journal of the bond market's daily action.
- Use barchart.com (or similar) to pull up the ZBU17 (adjusting for current/future contracts) on a 15-minute
timeframe for one day.
- This practice will increase your price action skills and develop a greater appreciation for this asset class,
commodities, and interest rates.
- You will also start studying its relationship with the dollar, gold, and foreign currencies as a whole.

,240
This has been an introduction to the bond market and one of the most basic concepts for analysis.
- The next lesson will cover split session rules.
